 # Dyalog APL Component File (.dcf) Format
 
 Reverse-engineered documentation of the binary format used by Dyalog APL 20.0 for component files.
 
 ## Documents
 
 | File | Description |
 |------|-------------|
 | [file-structure.md](file-structure.md) | Overall file layout and regions |
 | [header.md](header.md) | File header (bytes 0x00–0x47) |
 | [metadata-area.md](metadata-area.md) | Metadata/history area (bytes 0x48–0xFF, checksummed files) |
 | [component-blocks.md](component-blocks.md) | Component block format (primary and backup copies) |
 | [directory.md](directory.md) | Internal directory / FAT (paged B-tree, verified up to 3 levels) |
 | [footer.md](footer.md) | File footer, journal backup area, journaling protocol |
 | [free-block-tree.md](free-block-tree.md) | Free-block tree (FBT): BST for tracking freed space |
 | [file-locking.md](file-locking.md) | Byte-range file locking for FTIE/FSTIE coordination |
 | [io-protocol.md](io-protocol.md) | I/O protocol: exact API call sequences per operation and J level (Frida traces) |
 | [property-flags.md](property-flags.md) | Encoding of J/C/Z properties |
 | [crash-recovery.md](crash-recovery.md) | Crash recovery, corruption testing methodology, ⎕FCHK analysis |
 
 ## Key Concepts
 
 - **J** (Journaling level, 0–3): Controls transaction recovery. J≥1 adds journal area after footer. J≥2 requires C=1. **All J levels produce identical journal format**; differences are in fsync count only (0/1/2/3 — see [io-protocol.md](io-protocol.md)).
 - **C** (Checksum, 0–1): Enables integrity checksums on component blocks. Required by J≥2 and Z=1.
 - **Z** (Compression, 0–1): Enables LZ4 compression of component data. Requires C=1.
 
 ## File Variants by Size (2 components: `'hello'` and `⍳5`)
 
 | Properties | Size | Notes |
 |------------|------|-------|
 | J=0 C=0 Z=0 | 360 B | Minimal format |
 | J=0 C=1 Z=0 | 704 B | Checksums add ~2× overhead |
 | J=0 C=1 Z=1 | 880 B | Compression metadata adds 176 B |
 | J=1 C=0 Z=0 | 1080 B | Journaling without checksums |
 | J=1 C=1 Z=0 | 1240 B | Default Dyalog settings |
 | J=2 C=1 Z=0 | 1240 B | Same size as J=1 |
 | J=2 C=1 Z=1 | 1416 B | |
 | J=3 C=1 Z=1 | 1416 B | Maximum protection |
 
 ## Directory Tree Scaling
 
 | Levels | Component Range | Max Components |
 |--------|----------------|---------------|
 | 1 | 1–15 | 15 |
 | 2 | 16–255 | 255 |
 | 3 | 256–4,095 | 4,095 |
 | 4 | 4,096–65,535 | 65,535 |
 
 Formula: max at N levels = 16^N − 1 (branching factor 16, first slot reserved as sentinel).
 
 ## Format Versions
 
 - **32-bit (small-span)**: Bytes 2–3 = `08 01`. Uses 4-byte offsets/sizes. Legacy.
 - **64-bit (large-span)**: Bytes 2–3 = `14 00`. Uses 8-byte offsets/sizes. Current default.
 
 ## What's Known vs Unknown
 
 ### Fully Documented
 
 - File header (72 bytes) — all fields identified and verified; **state byte (0x07) = directory tree depth** (0–4)
 - Metadata area (184 bytes) — timestamps, checksums, compression flag
 - Component block structure — header, type codes, rank encoding, scalar optimization
 - **All ⎕DR data types mapped** — Boolean, Int8/16/32, Float64, Char8/16/32, Complex128, Nested/Pointer, Namespace (see [component-blocks.md](component-blocks.md))
 - **Nested array serialization** — recursive format with per-element size prefix; works to arbitrary depth
 - **Namespace serialization** — `220⌶` works directly on namespaces; round-trips through DCF files
 - **Higher-rank array shape encoding** — shape dimensions stored as consecutive u64 values; verified at ranks 1–4
 - **Boolean bit packing** — MSB-first ordering confirmed
 - **Integer type promotion** — boundaries at ±128, ±32768, ±2³¹; no Int64 (promotes to Float64)
 - **Checksum algorithm (C=1)** — Fletcher-32 variant with big-endian 16-bit word processing, `init=0xFFFF`; covers primary block + backup header; verified 63/63 test files (see [component-blocks.md](component-blocks.md))
 - Compression (Z=1) — **LZ4 raw block format**, same algorithm as `219⌶`; data stored as `220⌶` serialisation (see [component-blocks.md](component-blocks.md))
 - Directory/FAT — paged B-tree with branching factor 16, verified at 1/2/3/4 levels
 - Footer — 48-byte consistency marker with negative offset validation (confirmed by Frida)
 - Journal — redo logging protocol, sequence numbers, operation-specific behavior
 - Property encoding — lookup table for J/C in byte 1, Z at metadata 0xC8
 - Timestamp encoding — verified seconds+microseconds → ⎕FHIST 60ths conversion
 - I/O protocol — complete Frida-traced API call sequences for FAPPEND, FREPLACE (same/diff size), FDROP, FREAD, FHOLD at all J levels
 - File locking — byte-range locks at 0x40–0x43 fully mapped (tie, coordination, hold, shared-indicator)
 
 ### Partially Understood
 
 - Free-block tree metadata encoding — tree structure is known (BST by offset), but `left_meta`/`right_meta` fields use an unusual +1/+2 encoding (see [free-block-tree.md](free-block-tree.md))

 - `offset_B` (header 0x20) — always 0 in Dyalog 20.0 after exhaustive testing (5000 comps, 1.5MB files, many operations)
 
 ### Not Yet Investigated
 
 - Namespace serialization internal structure — the ~2KB payload format is complex (symbol tables, metadata, function definitions)
 - Decimal128 type (⎕DR 1287) — not observed in Dyalog 20.0; may require specific build options
 - Access matrix (⎕FSTAC) storage location and format
 - 32-bit (small-span) format details
 - Journal replay during in-flight operations — **CONFIRMED by Frida kill experiment** (see [crash-recovery.md](crash-recovery.md))
 
 ### Surprising Findings
 
 - **Metadata checksum is NOT validated** — corrupting offset 0x60 passes ⎕FCHK as CLEAN
 - **⎕FCHK doesn't fix component data** — reports REPAIRED for structural fixes but leaves damaged components as ERR:23
 - **Component checksum validated only at ⎕FREAD time** — not during ⎕FCHK
 - **Zeroed block magic causes WS FULL** — ⎕FREAD tries to parse garbage, allocates huge memory
 - **Journal sentinel (0xBBBBBBBB) is optional** — file without it reads fine
 - **Free-block tree root steals a freed block** — the FBT root is stored inside a freed block, and `end_offset` is repurposed to point to it
 - **Adjacent free blocks are automatically merged** — reduces tree nodes and consolidates space
 - **File locks use header sentinel bytes** — bytes 0x40 (FTIE) and 0x43 (FSTIE) are byte-range lock points, not pure data sentinels
 - **FTIE uses exclusive locks, FSTIE uses shared locks** — standard Windows `LockFileEx` semantics enable concurrent shared readers
 - **FHOLD uses byte 0x42 exclusive lock** — coordinates shared-tied processes; only effective when called from a defined function (immediate mode releases instantly)
 - **⎕FRDCI returns disk allocation size** — includes the 24-byte block header, not just data payload
 - **Byte 0x41 is a read/write coordination lock** — Frida traces revealed that byte 0x41 (previously "unused") is actively locked SHARED for reads and EXCLUSIVE for writes under FSTIE, providing read/write isolation (see [io-protocol.md](io-protocol.md))
 - **Header is double-read on every open** — lockless consistency check reads header twice to detect concurrent modifications
 - **Z=1 compression is standard LZ4** — uses the same `219⌶` / `220⌶` I-beam functions; descriptor at block+0x78 is the `219⌶` R[1] output verbatim; decompressible with standard `lz4.block.decompress()`
 - **Same-size FREPLACE at J=1 has zero FSYNCs** — journal backup is written but not synced, making it vulnerable to OS crashes
 - **FREPLACE and FDROP always use fewer FSYNCs than FAPPEND** — one fewer at every J level (0/0/1/2 vs 0/1/2/3) because old block/data remains intact as implicit backup
 - **Checksum is Fletcher-32 with big-endian word swap** — the reason standard Fletcher-32 tests failed is that the algorithm byte-swaps each 16-bit word to big-endian before accumulation, which is unusual in a little-endian file format
 - **No Int64 type exists** — integers ≥ 2³¹ are silently promoted to Float64 (⎕DR 645); the type byte sequence jumps from 0x24 (Int32) to 0x25 (Float64)
 - **220⌶ works directly on namespaces** — no `⎕OR` needed; namespaces round-trip through DCF files with member access preserved
 - **Namespace serialization is ~2KB minimum** — even an empty namespace carries interpreter metadata/symbol table overhead
 - **Boolean bits are MSB-first** — unusual for a little-endian format; the value `1 0 1 0 1 1 0 0` packs as byte `0xAC` (bit 7 first)
 - **State byte (0x07) encodes directory tree depth** — not just "has components"; values 0–4 map to 0–4 levels of the B-tree; never decreases after ⎕FDROP
 - **J≥1 files always write 256-byte header block** — header+metadata+journal pointers updated as a unit (vs 72 bytes for J=0)
 - **Journal commit point is a single 4-byte write** — the journal-active flag at offset 0x60 is the atomic boundary between rollback and forward recovery; everything before it can be discarded, everything after triggers redo replay
 - **Component data written BEFORE journal** — W1 writes the new component block, then W2 writes the journal backup; killed before journal commit → orphaned block ignored by ⎕FCHK
 - **Journal replay is idempotent** — even if the process dies after partial metadata commit but before clearing the journal pointer, ⎕FCHK safely replays the full journal again

<overview>
The user is reverse-engineering the Dyalog APL Component File (DCF) binary format, specifically the internal array serialization used by `220⌶` (Serialise Array). This session focused on completing the namespace serialization analysis: fixing the sub-array stream parser to correctly decode the internal structure of serialized namespaces, confirming the advance formula for size_words-prefixed sub-arrays, and mapping the complete three-section layout (names → values → internal metadata). The approach is experimental: create APL scripts that serialize controlled test values via `dyascript.exe`, then analyze the binary output with Python differential analysis.
</overview>

<history>
1. Session resumed from prior checkpoint where namespace binary analysis was in progress
   - Read `ns_analysis_output.txt` to find key diff patterns between NS variants
   - Discovered that same-size diffs (NS_A1 vs NS_AB1, NS_ABC1, NS_AFLOAT, NS_X42) showed precisely where member names and values are stored
   - Name at P+0xC0: UTF-16LE, only the name bytes differ between variants
   - Value at P+0xE0: only the type_code or value bytes differ

2. Created `analyze_ns_deep.py` for comprehensive structural analysis
   - Full hex dumps with u64 annotations, type code detection, UTF-16 string detection
   - Character table location finder (a-z in u32 LE)
   - Ran successfully, saved output to `ns_deep_output.txt`

3. Identified the sub-array stream structure starting at P+0x0098
   - Each namespace payload contains a stream of size_words-prefixed sub-arrays
   - Initial parsing showed names in reverse alphabetical order, then values, then metadata
   - Created `parse_ns_stream.py` for stream parsing

4. Debugged the advance formula for the sub-array stream
   - First attempt: `pos += (sw-1)*8` (advance only data, not sw word) — appeared to work for inner entries
   - Changed to `pos += sw*8` — BROKE parsing (jumped too far, read descriptors as size_words)
   - Systematic verification: tested both advance formulas at each position in NS_A1
   - **CONFIRMED: advance = (sw-1)*8, data = (sw-2)*8 bytes**
   - For sw=4: block is 24 bytes total (8 sw + 16 data), advance 24
   - For sw=7: block is 48 bytes total (8 sw + 40 data), advance 48
   - TERM (sw=0): advance 8

5. Fixed `parse_ns_stream.py` and ran complete analysis of all 13 variants
   - Successfully parsed all variants except NS_CHILD (which has embedded child NS with different marker)
   - Confirmed the three-section structure: NAMES → TERM → VALUES → TERM → INTERNAL_METADATA → 5×TERM

6. Verified header and metadata consistency across all 13 variants
   - Header (P+0x00..0x98): **identical across ALL 13 variants** — 152 bytes of fixed workspace metadata
   - Internal metadata (last 1752 bytes): **identical across ALL variants except NS_CHILD**
   - NS_CHILD has 728 differing metadata bytes (embeds child NS with its own metadata copy)

7. Was viewing `docs/component-blocks.md` to update with findings when compaction was triggered

<technical_details>
## Sub-Array Stream Format (CRITICAL FINDING)
The namespace payload (and nested array values) use a stream of size_words-prefixed sub-arrays:
```
[sw:u64] [data: (sw-2)*8 bytes]
```
- **advance** = `(sw-1) * 8` bytes from current position
- **data** = `(sw-2) * 8` bytes, starting at `pos + 8`
- **TERM** = sw=0, advance 8 bytes
- sw=1 is invalid (data would be negative)
- sw=4: 16 bytes data (2 words), 24 bytes total block
- sw=7: 40 bytes data (5 words), 48 bytes total block

This same format applies to nested array sub-elements AND namespace member entries.

## Complete Namespace Serialization Structure

### Preamble (bytes 0-9, before payload)
- Bytes 0-1: `DF A4` magic
- Bytes 2-5: u32 LE `size_words`
- Bytes 6-9: zeros

### Payload Layout
```
P+0x0000: type_code = 0x0000000000000000 (namespace marker)
P+0x0008: field_8 = 4 (constant)
P+0x0010: signature = 00 14 0E 14 00 00 00 00 (constant)
P+0x0018..0x0097: Fixed header (152 bytes, identical across all NS variants)
                   Contains workspace markers: 0x50D5, 0x5505, 0x58D5
                   Contains value 0x72 (='r'), value 14, value 8
P+0x0098: Start of sub-array stream
```

### Sub-Array Stream Sections
1. **NS_SELF_NAME**: `[sw=4] [01 00 00 88 ...] [FF FF 00 00 ...]` — always anonymous (0xFFFF)
2. **MEMBER_NAMES** (reverse-sorted alphabetically):
   - Each: `[sw=4+] [01 28 00 88 ...] [name in UTF-16LE ...]`
   - sw=4 for names ≤ 4 chars (16 bytes fits: 8-byte descriptor + 8-byte name slot)
   - sw=6 for "longname" (8 chars = 16 UTF-16 bytes → needs extra 16 bytes)
3. **TERM** (sw=0)
4. **MEMBER_VALUES** (same order as names, i.e., reverse-sorted):
   - Each is a standard 220⌶ sub-array: `[sw] [type_code] [count/value] [data...]`
   - Int8 scalar: sw=4, Float64 scalar: sw=4, Char8 "hello": sw=5, Int8[3]: sw=5, Bool[3]: sw=5
5. **TERM** (sw=0)
6. **INTERNAL METADATA** (~1752 bytes, identical across all simple NS variants):
   - Three `0x50D5` marker blocks (sw=7 each, 40 bytes data)
   - Type0x15 scalar with constant value `0x9b2ba1869b84063d` (possibly workspace hash/ID)
   - Various Int8 and Type0x14 scalars (workspace state)
   - Char8[0] empty string
   - Type0x14[256] + Int8[256] — character translation table (the APL atomic vector)
   - Type 0x1E scalar with value 1
   - Type0x14 scalar with value 0x285
   - Five TERM entries (sw=0) to end the stream

### Name Descriptor Format
```
01 28 00 88  — member name descriptor
01 00 00 88  — namespace self-name descriptor (followed by 0xFFFF for anonymous)
01 98 00 88  — child namespace member descriptor (seen in NS_CHILD)
```
Pattern: `01 TT 00 88` where TT indicates the member type:
- 0x00 = namespace self-name
- 0x28 = Char16 (regular variable name)
- 0x98 = child namespace reference

### Per-Member Overhead
Each member adds exactly 48 bytes for simple scalar values:
- 24 bytes for name entry (sw=4: 8-byte sw + 16-byte descriptor+name)
- 24 bytes for value entry (sw=4: 8-byte sw + 16-byte type+value)
Larger values (strings, vectors) add more in the value section.

### NS_CHILD Special Case
- Child namespace member uses descriptor `01 98 00 88` instead of `01 28 00 88`
- The child NS value embeds a complete namespace payload inline
- Uses marker `0x5505` instead of `0x0617` (Nested) in the D550 wrapper block
- Parser breaks at sw=1 inside the embedded NS (different internal structure)
- The metadata section has 728 bytes different from simple NS (contains child's own metadata copy)

## Decimal128 (⎕DR 1287) — Confirmed from prior checkpoint
- **Type byte = 0x2E** (decimal 46), NOT 0x2B as docs currently say
- Activation: `⎕FR←1287` BEFORE computation. Literals are parsed before ⎕FR takes effect.
- Element size: 16 bytes IEEE 754-2008 BID format
- BID: sign(1 bit) + exponent(14 bits, biased 6176) + coefficient(113 bits)

## Running Dyalog Scripts
```
D:\devel\dyalog\20.0\dyascript.exe "APLKEYS=D:\devel\dyalog\20.0\aplkeys" "APLTRANS=D:\devel\dyalog\20.0\apltrans" -script "script.apls"
```
- .apls files need UTF-8 BOM
- dyascript does NOT support ∇ function definitions — all code must be inline
- `⎕OR` fails in dyascript mode (DOMAIN ERROR)
- Use `:Trap 0 ... :Else ... :EndTrap` for error handling

## Type Byte Mapping (VERIFIED, needs doc update)
| Byte | ⎕DR | Type | Size |
|------|------|------|------|
| 0x21 | 11 | Boolean | 1 bit MSB-first |
| 0x22 | 83 | Int8 | 1 byte |
| 0x23 | 163 | Int16 | 2 bytes LE |
| 0x24 | 323 | Int32 | 4 bytes LE |
| 0x25 | 645 | Float64 | 8 bytes LE |
| 0x06 | 326 | Nested | variable recursive |
| 0x27 | 80 | Char8 | 1 byte |
| 0x28 | 160 | Char16 | 2 bytes LE |
| 0x29 | 320 | Char32 | 4 bytes LE |
| 0x2A | 1289 | Complex128 | 16 bytes |
| **0x2E** | **1287** | **Decimal128** | **16 bytes BID** |
| 0x00 | — | Namespace | variable ~2KB+ |

Additional type codes found inside namespace metadata:
- 0x14: Unknown internal type (used for workspace state values)
- 0x15: Unknown internal type (workspace hash/ID?)
- 0x1E: Unknown internal type (single scalar, value=1)
- 0x50D5, 0x5505, 0x58D5: Workspace structure markers (NOT type codes — raw u16 values in D550 header blocks)
</technical_details>

### Partially Understood

- Free-block tree metadata encoding — tree structure is known (BST by offset), but `left_meta`/`right_meta` fields use an unusual +1/+2 encoding (see [free-block-tree.md](free-block-tree.md))
- `first_comp` (header 0x08) — always 1 for fresh files, changes unpredictably after ⎕FDROP
- `alloc_size` (header 0x30) — diverges from `end_offset` after FDROP/FREPLACE operations
- `offset_B` (header 0x20) — always 0 in Dyalog 20.0 after exhaustive testing (5000 comps, 1.5MB files, many operations)

diff --git a/D:/_Utenti/stf.APLITA/Source/Repos/DCFFiles/docs/README.md b/D:/_Utenti/stf.APLITA/Source/Repos/DCFFiles/docs/README.md

+++ b/D:/_Utenti/stf.APLITA/Source/Repos/DCFFiles/docs/README.md
@@ -111,7 +111,8 @@
 - **Checksum is Fletcher-32 with big-endian word swap** — the reason standard Fletcher-32 tests failed is that the algorithm byte-swaps each 16-bit word to big-endian before accumulation, which is unusual in a little-endian file format
 - **No Int64 type exists** — integers ≥ 2³¹ are silently promoted to Float64 (⎕DR 645); the type byte sequence jumps from 0x24 (Int32) to 0x25 (Float64)
 - **220⌶ works directly on namespaces** — no `⎕OR` needed; namespaces round-trip through DCF files with member access preserved
-- **Namespace serialization is ~2KB minimum** — even an empty namespace carries interpreter metadata/symbol table overhead
+- **Namespace member names are stored in reverse alphabetical order** — appears related to APL's internal symbol table ordering; values follow the same order
+- **Namespace serialization is ~2KB minimum** — even an empty namespace carries 152 bytes of fixed header plus ~1752 bytes of interpreter metadata (character translation tables, workspace state)
 - **Boolean bits are MSB-first** — unusual for a little-endian format; the value `1 0 1 0 1 1 0 0` packs as byte `0xAC` (bit 7 first)
 - **State byte (0x07) encodes directory tree depth** — not just "has components"; values 0–4 map to 0–4 levels of the B-tree; never decreases after ⎕FDROP
 - **J≥1 files always write 256-byte header block** — header+metadata+journal pointers updated as a unit (vs 72 bytes for J=0)

1. # Plan: Namespace Serialization Deep-Dive & Decimal128
2. 
3. ## Status of Prior Work (COMPLETED)
4. All basic array types, nested arrays, and simple namespace round-trips are fully mapped.
5. See `docs/component-blocks.md` for complete documentation.
6. 
7. ## Phase A: Decimal128 Activation (⎕FR←1287)
8. 
9. Prior attempt failed — `⎕FR←1287` still produced Float64. The ⎕FR docs explain why:
10. > "numeric constants entered into the Session are evaluated (and assigned a data type)
11. > before the line is actually executed"
12. 
13. **Fix**: Use arithmetic (`1÷3`, `0+1.5`) to produce Decimal128 values at runtime, 
14. not literal constants. The `⎕FR` value at computation time determines the result type.
15. 
16. ### Experiment: `probe_decimal128.apls`
17. 1. Set `⎕FR←1287` on a separate line
18. 2. Compute values via arithmetic: `x←1÷3`, `y←0+1.5 2.5`
19. 3. Verify with `⎕DR x` — should show 1287, not 645
20. 4. If successful, serialize with `1(220⌶)` and dump bytes
21. 5. Look for type byte 0x2B
22. 6. Write to DCF file and verify round-trip
23. 
24. ## Phase B: Namespace Serialization Deep-Dive
25. 
26. ### What We Already Know

 # Plan: Namespace Serialization Deep-Dive & Decimal128
 
 ## Status of Prior Work (COMPLETED)
 All basic array types, nested arrays, and simple namespace round-trips are fully mapped.
 See `docs/component-blocks.md` for complete documentation.
 
 ## Phase A: Decimal128 Activation (⎕FR←1287)
 
 Prior attempt failed — `⎕FR←1287` still produced Float64. The ⎕FR docs explain why:
 > "numeric constants entered into the Session are evaluated (and assigned a data type)
 > before the line is actually executed"
 
 **Fix**: Use arithmetic (`1÷3`, `0+1.5`) to produce Decimal128 values at runtime, 
 not literal constants. The `⎕FR` value at computation time determines the result type.
 
 ### Experiment: `probe_decimal128.apls`
 1. Set `⎕FR←1287` on a separate line
 2. Compute values via arithmetic: `x←1÷3`, `y←0+1.5 2.5`
 3. Verify with `⎕DR x` — should show 1287, not 645
 4. If successful, serialize with `1(220⌶)` and dump bytes
 5. Look for type byte 0x2B
 6. Write to DCF file and verify round-trip
 
 ## Phase B: Namespace Serialization Deep-Dive
 
 ### What We Already Know
 From the raw hex dump of `test_ns.dcf` (ns.x=42, ns.name='hello', ns.vec=⍳10):
 
 - Type_code = 0x0000 (unique namespace marker)
 - Member names visible in UTF-16LE at +0x00C8 ('x'), +0x00E0 ('vec'), +0x00F8 ('name')
 - Member values at +0x0110 ('hello' as Char8 vec), +0x0130 (⍳10 as Int8 vec), +0x0158 (42 as Int8 scalar)
 - Values use standard 220⌶ payload format (size_words prefix + type_code + data)
 - ~2KB of metadata/overhead surrounds the member data
 - Huge character table at +0x0300..+0x0800 (contains ASCII, APL symbols in u32 LE)
 
 ### Strategy: Differential Analysis
 Create many controlled namespace variants, changing one thing at a time. Binary-diff the serialized output to isolate each structural element.
 
 ### Experiments (`probe_ns_deep.apls`)
 
 1. **Empty NS** — baseline structure, identify all metadata
 2. **NS with 1 Int8 member** (`a←1`) — see where single member appears
 3. **NS with 2 Int8 members** (`a←1`, `b←2`) — identify member table growth
 4. **NS with 1 member, longer name** (`longname←1`) — isolate name length encoding
 5. **NS with 1 Float64 member** (`a←1.5`) — see if value type changes structure
 6. **NS with 1 String member** (`a←'hello'`) — string value
 7. **NS with nested member** (`a←(1 2)(3 4)`) — nested value inside NS
 8. **NS with sub-namespace** (`child←⎕NS '' ⋄ child.x←1`) — recursive NS
 9. **NS with 5 members** — measure per-member overhead
 
 ### Analysis Script (`analyze_ns.py`)
 
 For each pair of serializations:
 - Identify byte ranges that changed
 - Correlate changes with member names/values/types
 - Map the internal structure:
   - Header/metadata region
   - Member name table (UTF-16LE strings)
   - Member value table (220⌶ payloads)
   - Symbol table / character table
   - Any index/pointer structures
   - Trailing metadata
 
 ### Deliverables
 - `probe_decimal128.apls` — Decimal128 experiment
 - `probe_ns_deep.apls` — Comprehensive NS serialization experiments
 - `analyze_ns.py` — Python binary analysis/diffing tool
 - Updated `docs/component-blocks.md` with NS format specification

# Plan: Namespace Serialization Deep-Dive & Decimal128

## Status of Prior Work (COMPLETED)
All basic array types, nested arrays, and simple namespace round-trips are fully mapped.
See `docs/component-blocks.md` for complete documentation.

## Phase A: Decimal128 Activation (⎕FR←1287)

Prior attempt failed — `⎕FR←1287` still produced Float64. The ⎕FR docs explain why:
> "numeric constants entered into the Session are evaluated (and assigned a data type)
> before the line is actually executed"

**Fix**: Use arithmetic (`1÷3`, `0+1.5`) to produce Decimal128 values at runtime, 
not literal constants. The `⎕FR` value at computation time determines the result type.

### Experiment: `probe_decimal128.apls`
1. Set `⎕FR←1287` on a separate line
2. Compute values via arithmetic: `x←1÷3`, `y←0+1.5 2.5`
3. Verify with `⎕DR x` — should show 1287, not 645
4. If successful, serialize with `1(220⌶)` and dump bytes
5. Look for type byte 0x2B
6. Write to DCF file and verify round-trip

## Phase B: Namespace Serialization Deep-Dive

### What We Already Know
From the raw hex dump of `test_ns.dcf` (ns.x=42, ns.name='hello', ns.vec=⍳10):

- Type_code = 0x0000 (unique namespace marker)
- Member names visible in UTF-16LE at +0x00C8 ('x'), +0x00E0 ('vec'), +0x00F8 ('name')
- Member values at +0x0110 ('hello' as Char8 vec), +0x0130 (⍳10 as Int8 vec), +0x0158 (42 as Int8 scalar)
- Values use standard 220⌶ payload format (size_words prefix + type_code + data)
- ~2KB of metadata/overhead surrounds the member data
- Huge character table at +0x0300..+0x0800 (contains ASCII, APL symbols in u32 LE)

### Strategy: Differential Analysis
Create many controlled namespace variants, changing one thing at a time. Binary-diff the serialized output to isolate each structural element.

### Experiments (`probe_ns_deep.apls`)

1. **Empty NS** — baseline structure, identify all metadata
2. **NS with 1 Int8 member** (`a←1`) — see where single member appears
3. **NS with 2 Int8 members** (`a←1`, `b←2`) — identify member table growth
4. **NS with 1 member, longer name** (`longname←1`) — isolate name length encoding
5. **NS with 1 Float64 member** (`a←1.5`) — see if value type changes structure
6. **NS with 1 String member** (`a←'hello'`) — string value
7. **NS with nested member** (`a←(1 2)(3 4)`) — nested value inside NS
8. **NS with sub-namespace** (`child←⎕NS '' ⋄ child.x←1`) — recursive NS
9. **NS with 5 members** — measure per-member overhead

### Analysis Script (`analyze_ns.py`)

For each pair of serializations:
- Identify byte ranges that changed
- Correlate changes with member names/values/types
- Map the internal structure:
  - Header/metadata region
  - Member name table (UTF-16LE strings)
  - Member value table (220⌶ payloads)
  - Symbol table / character table
  - Any index/pointer structures
  - Trailing metadata

### Deliverables
- `probe_decimal128.apls` — Decimal128 experiment
- `probe_ns_deep.apls` — Comprehensive NS serialization experiments
- `analyze_ns.py` — Python binary analysis/diffing tool
- Updated `docs/component-blocks.md` with NS format specification
arguments.new_str:
# Plan: Namespace Serialization Deep-Dive & Decimal128

## ALL PHASES COMPLETE ✓

### Phase A: Decimal128 (⎕FR←1287) — DONE ✓
- Type byte confirmed as **0x2E** (not 0x2B)
- Activation: `⎕FR←1287` must be set BEFORE arithmetic (literals parsed before ⎕FR takes effect)
- 16-byte IEEE 754-2008 BID format
- Docs updated in `component-blocks.md`

### Phase B: Namespace Serialization — DONE ✓
- Sub-array stream format fully decoded: `[sw:u64][data:(sw-2)*8 bytes]`, advance=(sw-1)*8
- Four-section stream: NS_SELF_NAME → MEMBER_NAMES(reverse-sorted) → VALUES → INTERNAL_METADATA
- Name descriptors: 0x00=self, 0x28=variable, 0x98=child NS
- Per-scalar-member overhead: exactly 48 bytes (24 name + 24 value)
- Internal metadata: ~1752 bytes containing APL ⎕AV character translation tables + workspace state
- All 13 NS variants parsed successfully by `parse_ns_stream.py`

### Documentation — DONE ✓
- `docs/component-blocks.md`: Full namespace format spec + Decimal128 fix
- `docs/README.md`: Updated "Fully Documented" / "Partially Understood" / "Surprising Findings"

### Possible Future Work
- Decode namespace internal metadata type codes (0x14, 0x15, 0x1E)
- NS with functions (⎕FX) — dyascript doesn't support ∇ definitions
- NS_CHILD embedded format (sw=1 entry breaks current parser)
- NS with 10+ members to verify scaling
